stem: A hospital uses a CT scan screening to screen for early-stage lung cancer. The test has a sensitivity of 95% and a specificity of 88%. The prevalence of early-stage lung cancer in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.5% and 3.6%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has early-stage lung cancer? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(early-stage lung cancer | positive test) ranges from 0.108 to 0.228 as prevalence varies from 1.5% to 3.6%. Point estimate at 2.5% prevalence: 0.169.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.12.
The confidence interval [0.108, 0.228] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (95%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
